title: Spinning on a supported spindle
subtitle: Spinning fine singles with the spindle resting in a bowl rather than hanging free
excerpt: The supported spindle rests in a bowl or on a smooth surface rather than hanging free like a drop spindle. Because the spindle bears its own weight, the spinner can use much finer fibre and spin much thinner yarn than a drop spindle allows. Supported spinning is standard practice for fine cashmere, qiviut, baby camel, and fine Rambouillet; it is also used by tradition in Bolivian, Andean, and parts of Tibetan textile practice.
sourceNotes: Supported spindle spinning is a long-established tradition in Andean, Tibetan, and South Asian textile practice. Technique is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A spindle that rests in a bowl or against a smooth surface during use, rather than hanging free. The spindle's weight is borne by the support, allowing very fine drafting without the downward pull that limits drop-spindle fineness.
Yarn spun from fibre without plying. A single ply. Singles can be used directly as lace-weight or weaving yarn, or plied with another single to produce a stable 2-ply yarn.
The wound mass of spun yarn on the spindle shaft. A well-formed cop is conical and balanced so the spindle spins evenly.

content.text: rests in a smooth ceramic bowl held between the knees or placed on a cloth in the lap. The dominant hand spins the shaft; the other hand drafts the fibre. Because neither hand must support the spindle's weight, both are free for fine, controlled drafting. The
build up on the shaft as a
rather than hanging below the spindle as with drop-spindle work.

attrs.title: Wool dust
attrs.body: Carding or combing fibre before supported spinning throws fine dust. Work in a ventilated room. People with sensitive lungs should wear a dust mask.

items.name: Smooth ceramic bowl (for the spindle tip to rest in)
Niddy-noddy (for winding off into a hank)

content.text: Tie a leader thread (a short piece of any yarn, about 30 cm) to the shaft just above the whorl. Wrap it up the shaft several times, then leave a 10-cm length free. Attach the fibre to this free end by overlapping it and rolling the join between your fingers.
Step 2: Set the spindle in the bowl and start spinning

Rest the spindle tip in the bowl. Spin the shaft by rolling it against your thigh or between the fingers of the dominant hand (different spindle traditions use different spinning methods: roll against the thigh for Andean-tradition spindles; roll between finger and thumb for fine Tibetan spindles). The twist runs up into the fibre as you draft.

With the non-dominant hand, pinch the fibre supply just above the twist entry point and pull (draft) upward, allowing fibres to slide between the fingers. For fine yarn, draft slowly with minimal fibre in hand. The spindle tip may drift in the bowl; nudge it back to centre without stopping the drafting.

When 20 to 30 cm of spun yarn has accumulated, wind it onto the shaft, building a cone shape from just above the whorl upward. The cop must be symmetrical for the spindle to remain balanced. A lopsided cop wobbles in the bowl and produces uneven twist.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems when learning supported-spindle spinning.

items.symptom: Spindle tip keeps skipping out of the bowl
items.cause: Bowl surface too rough or too flat; spindle tip grinding rather than rolling.
items.fix: Use a smooth-glazed ceramic bowl. A ceramic tile or a wooden bowl with a light wax finish also works. The tip should spin freely without friction.

items.symptom: Yarn breaks at the draft zone repeatedly
items.cause: Over-twisted singles. Too much twist entering too short a draft zone.
items.fix: Draft a longer length before allowing the twist to enter. Slow the spinning action. The twist should travel only as far as the pinch, not above it.
